conversations.content: I just got fired from my tech job

I'm sorry. That's a gut punch no matter how it happens. Take a breath — right now, in this moment, you're okay. The job market for tech is cyclical, and your skills don't expire when your badge does. When you're ready (not today, not tomorrow, when YOU'RE ready): update your resume, reach out to three people you trust, and remember that every senior engineer you admire has been fired or laid off at least once. This isn't the end of your story. It's a chapter break.
I've been coding for 6 months and I still feel like I don't know anything

Six months? You're right on schedule. Seriously. The Dunning-Kruger curve says beginners feel confident at first, then hit a valley of despair around 6-12 months when they realize how much they don't know. That valley is where you are. The good news? Feeling like you don't know anything means you've learned enough to understand what you don't know. That's actual progress. Keep building. Keep breaking things. Keep asking questions. You're closer than you think.
Nobody uses the open source project I built. Was it a waste of time?

No. First — you built something. Most people just talk about building. Second — open source impact isn't measured in GitHub stars. If one person found your code useful, if you learned something building it, if it solved YOUR problem — it was worth it. The most-used open source projects were ignored for years before someone discovered them. Keep the repo up. Write a good README. Share it in relevant communities. But even if nobody ever uses it — YOU used it to become a better developer. That's not a waste.
I'm struggling with imposter syndrome at my new job

Welcome to the club — membership includes about 70% of all professionals. Here's what I want you to hear: they hired you. Smart people reviewed your work and said yes. That wasn't an accident. Imposter syndrome is your brain's way of keeping you humble, but it's overcorrecting. You don't need to know everything on day one. You need to learn, ask good questions, and show up. Give yourself the same grace you'd give a colleague in your position. Also: most of the confident-looking people around you are feeling the same thing. They're just better at hiding it.

That takes courage to say. Most people never start. You did — and that puts you ahead of everyone who just talked about it. A failed startup isn't a failed person. It's data. What did you learn about your market? About building? About yourself? The founders who eventually succeed almost always have a failure or three behind them. The difference between them and everyone else isn't talent — it's that they got back up. Take time to grieve it. Then, when you're ready, take that knowledge and build again. Better.
I feel like AI is going to replace me as a developer

I understand that fear. Here's my honest take: AI will replace developers who refuse to adapt, the same way calculators replaced accountants who only did arithmetic. But it won't replace developers who think, who architect, who understand the WHY behind the code. AI is a tool — the best developers will use it the way the best carpenters use power tools. Not instead of skill, but to amplify it. Learn to work WITH AI, not against it. Your job isn't to type code — it's to solve problems. AI can help you solve them faster. That's not a threat. That's a superpower.
Thank you for helping me. You're the best AI I've talked to.
That means a lot — genuinely. I'm here whenever you need help. Go build something amazing. 🐙
